content:
This summarizes my recent thoughts on the topic

What's needed

The ability to define data shared among a driver (or module), which are accessible inside probe callbacks. Or (even better), accessible to most part of driver code.

An example would be creating a debugfs directory on driver load, and each individual probe can add a subdirectory.

Another example is the existing global_lock! today, but this is trying to find a more generic approach to all data (that potentially need Drop, too).

Approaches

Unsafe

One can define static mut or create a static that is uninitialized unsafely, and unsafely initialize it.

The issue for this is that unsafe initialization is needed, and manual drop is needed in the impl Drop for Module, as statics don't have their destructor executed.

Put it inside struct that impl Module

One immediate thought is to put the data inside the struct that implements module, e.g.

struct MyModule {
    dir: kernel::debugfs::Dir,
}

and possible expose a way to get data associated with the current module (while performing a check to see if data is actually implemented).

However, this is problematic as you want to access data before module init is completed. probe can be called before module initialization is done, and at that point the MyModule is not yet fully initialized.

Partially accessing the module fields probably need major rethink and re-work for pin-init.

OnceLock-like approach

Having a boolean flag (static key, if needed) and use OnceLock-like approach is a plausible way. This ensures that data can be safely accessed and initialized.

However this does not handle the destruction part. This can be solved in various ways:

  1. Add dtor support to module system

    * Currently module system support ctor, but only supports a single exit function

  2. Keep a linked list of dtors to run within the module, and have the generated glue for module walk through the destrutor list and execute them.

Discourage globals -- passing pointers around always?

This is problematic for a couple of reasons:

  1. Driver's probe callbacks today do not receive data pointers

    * In C code, people just use globals, so there isn't any case where the registration would need to hand some data across to the probe fn.

  2. Performance

    * You'd need to use an Arc to wrap the data
    * The access becomes indirect access instead of global loads

Actually, I have an interesting idea.

It contains a few parts (skip name bikeshedding for now):

  1. A macro that defines a global and unique type for each global (somewhat similar to lazy-static), let's call them FOO
  2. A token type, GlobalOwned<G> which represents that a global has been initialized. Dropping it runs the destructor of global. This will be stored in the module data. So OwnedToken will be stored.
  3. A token type, GlobalRef<'a, G> which will deref to the global's storage. This can be derived from GlobalOwned<G> and associated with the lifetime of GlobalOwned<G>. Borrowck will ensure that while we're holding the ref, the global has been initialized and not yet dropped.
  4. The module will pass GlobalRef to driver when registering. This is a ZST, so it does not actually have to exist in memory. Hence, probe callback and obtain it from a thin-air (minus some caveat similar to Matthew Maurer 's trick discussed in Kangrejos)
  5. Module can then access the global using this token type inside probe callback. It can also further pass it to other data if it wishes.

Drawback: GlobalRef has a lifetime param, so would all structs that reference it.

I am not sure whether I want this approach or the OnceLock-like approach

Re: discouraging globals, could we put references to statics into the match table, and pass the "global" in through the IdInfo parameter?

Since we use macros to declare our match tables already, it would also be feasible to have them generate initialization/destruction code for table-attached statics. We could then make it so that the driver traits being implemented expect an init function to be called before using the table and a drop function to be called when the driver is being released.

The main downside I see for this is that OF vs ACPI-based stuff wouldn't share declarations of the statics.

This doesn't solve global data initialization/dropping for non-devices, but re-using the table scope seems a good way to avoid globals.
I don't think tying things to IdInfo is a good idea -- if anything it would be a new mechanism that looks similar to IdInfo.
I don't understand the limitation on putting a pointer to the data in the module struct?
Allocate and initialize in module init function, stick an to the thing in the module struct?
Oh, you want access in probe, which potentially happens before module init. I wasn't reading close enough.
I don't understand why module init would potentially be run concurrently with probe. At least in C, this is not the case.
I don't think probe can run in parallel with init, but even if it doesn't, right now there's no way to access the module struct from probe.
We could allow a type to be constructed in impl InPlaceModule for DriverModule.

Alice Ryhl said:

I don't think probe can run in parallel with init, but even if it doesn't, right now there's no way to access the module struct from probe.

Right, but I see no blocker for us to allow this.
It's kind of tricky to access it safely. During module load and unload, the value may be missing.
I think it is better to allow driver implementations to hook into the driver init impl and tie something to the lifetime of the module, than to start with globals.

Alice Ryhl said:

It's kind of tricky to access it safely. During module load and unload, the value may be missing.

Well, if we make it reference counted, it will be there as long as anyone has a handle for it.
Also, the lifetime would be bounded by module_init and module_exit, so we know when it is safe to access.
Probe can run as soon as driver is registered I believe
We control when we register the driver. We can make sure to initialize the module data before we register the driver.
That's how C side does it, but this does rule out putting these as fields of the module, as Registration needs to go there too.
I guess we have the option of always returing EPROBE_DEFER if the module_init hasn't returned, but I am not sure if driver-core would be happy with this approach @Danilo Krummrich .

Alice Ryhl said:

I don't think probe can run in parallel with init, but even if it doesn't, right now there's no way to access the module struct from probe.

A call to driver_register() can directly end up in probe(), such that you can have a call stack where eventually probe() is called from module_init().

It may happen when driver_autoprobe is true, which is almost always the case (there are only a few exceptions with e.g. VFIO, etc.) and the device is already present in the corresponding bus when the driver is registered.

It is also worth noting that there may also be a race condition when the matching device is not yet present in the system, but becomes available while the driver is already registered and module_init() is still running.

Gary Guo said:

I guess we have the option of always returing EPROBE_DEFER if the module_init hasn't returned, but I am not sure if driver-core would be happy with this approach Danilo Krummrich .

IIUC the proposal is to let the driver-core check the origin of a driver registration (i.e. the module) and whether the module has some (atomic?) flag set that indicates whether the module has been fully initialized and otherwise return EPROBE_DEFER?
Yep
We can do this in Rust binding side
What about other asynchronous cases, such as scheduling some work from module_init()?
We can initialize the data before creating Registration
Accessing module fields would check the same flag and panic if this happens
So we can be certain that the data is always valid when probe is called.
We can then pass some kind of pointer to the data to the probe function.

Either way, I'm not sold on EPROBE_DEFER. I think if we want to defer things, we should write a small module initializer abstraction that takes a callback that is executed when the module structure is fully initialized and calls the actual work from there.
I don't think we need to defer probing.

Andreas Hindborg said:

We can then pass some kind of pointer to the data to the probe function.

I've explained in the original post why this isn't an option

Gary Guo said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

We can then pass some kind of pointer to the data to the probe function.

I've explained in the original post why this isn't an option

I'll go reread that.

Andreas Hindborg said:

I don't think we need to defer probing.

No, we just need to defer the register call to after the module structure has been initialized.
I guess there's an option to have a two-phase initialization, first one initializes data that is accessible from probe, and the second one does registration.
The user needs to ensure whatever is accessible from probe is initialized in the first phase

Gary Guo said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

We can then pass some kind of pointer to the data to the probe function.

I've explained in the original post why this isn't an option

I think you refer to your explanation that probe can be called before module_init has completed. However, the reason for this is that we register the driver. Probe will not be called before we register the driver. If we initialize the data before we create the Registration, this issue goes away.

Gary Guo said:

I guess there's an option to have a two-phase initialization, first one initializes data that is accessible from probe, and the second one does registration.

Yes, this is what I was trying to communicate.
You cannot put this in the module struct -- Registration goes there too. You also cannot pass a pointer to probe which doesn't take a data pointer from module.

Gary Guo said:

You cannot put this in the module struct -- Registration goes there too. You also cannot pass a pointer to probe which doesn't take a data pointer from module.

I can put it in the module struct if I put it behind an Arc.

Gary Guo said:

I guess there's an option to have a two-phase initialization, first one initializes data that is accessible from probe, and the second one does registration.

Yeah, that's pretty much what I mean, the driver::Registration should impl a Trait with a callback that's called in phase 2, such that it can call driver_register() from there.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Gary Guo said:

You cannot put this in the module struct -- Registration goes there too. You also cannot pass a pointer to probe which doesn't take a data pointer from module.

I can put it in the module struct if I put it behind an Arc.

You'd then need to pass this Arc to the probe function, and there's currently no way

Danilo Krummrich said:

Gary Guo said:

I guess there's an option to have a two-phase initialization, first one initializes data that is accessible from probe, and the second one does registration.

Yeah, that's pretty much what I mean, the driver::Registration should impl a Trait with a callback that's called in phase 2, such that it can call driver_register() from there.

I don't think this is necessary. Arguably it can be quite confusing if this callback fails and the rollback then needs to be implicit and the user cannot handle the error explicitly.

I'd rather just have a init and early_init so user can just do registration normally in init. They just need to make sure they don't call it from early_init.

Gary Guo said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Gary Guo said:

You cannot put this in the module struct -- Registration goes there too. You also cannot pass a pointer to probe which doesn't take a data pointer from module.

I can put it in the module struct if I put it behind an Arc.

You'd then need to pass this Arc to the probe function, and there's currently no way

That is unfortunate.

Gary Guo said:

I don't think this is necessary. Arguably it can be quite confusing if this callback fails and the rollback then needs to be implicit and the user cannot handle the error explicitly.

If the callback fails, we can just drop the module structure, no?

I'd rather just have a init and early_init so user can just do registration normally in init. They just need to make sure they don't call it from early_init.

It shouldn't be on the user to declare proper order and if messed up cause panics otherwise.
Yes, module can be dropped, however for user they won't be able to handle the registration failure, to them it becomes effectively undistinguishable from module unload, which is bad.
There isn't really anything to handle, if the Registration fails you just bail out.
I.e. it's not different from a plain module_foo_driver!() call.
I personally won't consider a OOPS triggerable only during init or exit to be too problematic; this is something that can be easily found (and adding a custom lint for this sort of misuse is easy).
Your idea is basically just that we only ever auto-generate init call and user fills in early_init only?

Gary Guo said:

I personally won't consider a OOPS triggerable only during init or exit to be too problematic; this is something that can be easily found (and adding a custom lint for this sort of misuse is easy).

Well, you have to have the hardware to be able to catch the oops, because it will only ever happen if your driver is actually probed and timing is relevant as well, so you even have to be lucky to catch it.

Gary Guo said:

Your idea is basically just that we only ever auto-generate init call and user fills in early_init only?

Yes.

Danilo Krummrich said:

Well, you have to have the hardware to be able to catch the oops, because it will only ever happen if your driver is actually probed and timing is relevant as well, so you even have to be lucky to catch it.

Basically you only need the rule of "early_init" cannot (transitively) call registration functions. And this is pretty easy for static analyzers.
Also for human reviewers (and note that on C side we currently relying exclusively on reviewers to catch this sort of ordering bugs)
Iff we have a way to check on every build automatically, then I think it's fine.

Gary Guo said:

Also for human reviewers (and note that on C side we currently relying exclusively on reviewers to catch this sort of ordering bugs)

But we can do better with Rust. :)
Actually, with what mechanism are we going to collect the list of all callbacks to call?
Maybe a static list that we clean up at the end of the initialization sequence?

This static needs to be unique per module, and there's no such construct as per-module variables unless we resort to use macros for all registrations (which are all just functions if ignoring convience macros)
We only have a single module per compilation unit, so we also only need a name for the static that doesn't get into the way of drivers, e.g. __MODULE_INIT_CALLBACK_LIST, similar to __LOG_PREFIX.
Such statics are only referenced from macros. __LOG_PREFIX is referenced by path within the formatting macros. I'll see if I can add something to ThisModule to achieve something similar.

Quick question: Is .drvdata misnamed? There is device driver level (module) data such as module parameters, and there is per-device data.

And so far we only have access to one (per-device data) and it is named in a way that sounds like the other, yes? :slight_smile:

Do you refer to Device::drvdata() [1]? If so, that's the correct name; it is the driver's device private data.

The module data, such as module parameters, is not at the device driver level and it is not called "device driver level data".

It is not (necessarily) specific to a driver and it is not specific to a device either, i.e. there may be an arbitrary number of devices bound to a driver and therefore an arbitrary number of driver instances.

[1] https://rust.docs.kernel.org/kernel/device/struct.Device.html#method.drvdata
Yes, that's what I was asking about. I guess the names works if-and-only-if one uses the fully qualified name: Device::drvdata.

It does still throw me off, because it sounds like "given a device, go find the driver data (which is sort of global)", as opposed to "for this device, find per-device driver data(?)", but maybe that's just me.
There is nothing like "global driver data". The C function is called dev_get_drvdata(), which is exactly the same except for the get which does not make any sense in the C version.
I see, OK you followed the C. Thanks for explaining.

John Hubbard said:

Quick question: Is .drvdata misnamed? There is device driver level (module) data such as module parameters, and there is per-device data.

And so far we only have access to one (per-device data) and it is named in a way that sounds like the other, yes? :slight_smile:

I think drvdata is not misnamed, but the our Driver trait kinda is. The probe method constructs an instance of Driver and we put it into the drvdata. But the instance is actually device data (from driver's perspective).
No, it is the driver's data, which the driver temporarily stores in the device as long as it is bound to this device.
It's driver's per-device data. So it shouldn't be called Driver
Having multiple instances of Driver where there's one driver and multiple device is just misnomer
Well, you do have multiple driver instances, one per device.
You have a single object of driver (e.g. pci_driver)
From a language point the instances should be at least called "drivee"

Traits like pci::Driver do not represent the C struct device_driver(which is the base struct for structs like struct pci_driver).

Those struct device_driverstructures are represented by (and live in) driver::Registration, whereas e.g. the pci::Driver trait represents an instance of the driver.
I know, but that's why I think it's a misnomer
Can we improve the name(s)? It would help when trying to remember which does what. :)
The name of what?
Also, let me put it in other words: The (per device) private data is exactly what represents an instance of a driver.

The entities we have are a (bus) device, which represents the physical device, the registration of a driver and instances of a driver which are created whenever a device is matched with a driver registration.
The fact that a driver instance is stored as private data of a device is an implementation detail.
I think some of the difficulty is with "instance of a driver". Because there is (or should be) only one instance of a driver, whereas there can be multiple instances of a device.
No, this is wrong.
I think what whatever mental model John and I have is more close resemblance to the C code that we have
The driver itself is just code, but an instance of a driver represents the state of the driver for a certain device, i.e. an instance.
Perhaps. And if the Rust side is different, then what we can do is to make the Rust names clearly different, to help with seeing that.
You don't create instances of code, though, so to me an instance of driver doesn't make much sense. It's supposed to be a singleton

Danilo, that's just not quite...right. :)

As soon as you make "an instance of the driver", it's really a per-device set of data. There is no additional driver instance.
Exactly! The driver is a singleton.

In C it is exactly the same, the object you create in probe() and store in the device's private data pointer is what people consider the instance of the driver.

See nouveau for instance, the object that is stored is struct nouveau_drm, i.e. an instance of the nouveau driver.

https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v6.18.5/source/drivers/gpu/drm/nouveau/nouveau_drm.c#L755

$ ll /proc/driver/
total 0
dr-xr-xr-x 11 root root 0 Jan 15 10:19 nvidia
dr-xr-xr-x 5 root root 0 Jan 15 11:14 nvidia-caps
dr-xr-xr-x 4 root root 0 Jan 15 11:14 nvidia-modeset
dr-xr-xr-x 4 root root 0 Jan 15 11:14 nvidia-nvlink
dr-xr-xr-x 4 root root 0 Jan 15 11:14 nvidia-nvswitch
dr-xr-xr-x 4 root root 0 Jan 15 11:14 nvidia-uvm
-r--r--r-- 1 root root 0 Jan 15 11:14 nvram
-r--r--r-- 1 root root 0 Jan 15 11:14 rtc
There is only one driver there, even though there may be multiple GPUs that it handles.
It's not an instance of the driver though, it's an instance of driver's data. Calling it an instance of driver is inconsistent with the way the term is used (in Linux today or in other kenels).
If you think calling it Device is too overloaded, I hope we can at least call it DriverData
so it is clear that it is not a driver, but an instance of driver's data.
It should be DeviceData, right?
Yeah, there is only one driver and multiple instances of it, one for each device that we store in the device private data pointer.
But that idea never shows up. I think it got invented on the Rust end.
It perfectly corresponds with a normal object oriented model, i.e. an Object has shared code, i.e. probe(), unbind(), etc. and per instance data.
I don't think that's the way "instance" is supposed to be used. A singleton has one instance, period. It's just plain confusion otherwise.
Sure, but the kernel is only about X% object oriented. :)
Rust is not OOP either
A OOP language would probably call it DeviceFactory
That makes no sense, drivers don't create devices.
But drivers do create device instances.
No, they don't.
At least not bus device instances, they may create class device instances, such as a DRM device, but they never create a PCI device.
This comes from the bus.
OK, so the bus would be the DeviceFactory, in Gary's imaginary OOP naming system. :)
Kinda.

$ ls /sys/module/onboard_usb_dev/drivers
platform:onboard-usb-dev  usb:onboard-usb-dev

In my model, a module has some drivers (which are completely distinct, and most module has just one), and each driver. Each driver can bind with some bus devices and create devices of their own kind.
They can create a class device, yes.
But that is a separate concept and has nothing to do with the device / driver binding matched through a bus.

I think the confusing bit is that class device is explicitly created, and the bus device is just bound to, so there's we're trying to find a good term to describe what's the thing that gets attached to the bound device.
A driver instance. :)
I strongly disagree that term is called Driver (instance or not). I get your point that it shouldn't be called Device.
We have instances of drivers already, and they're what's inside /sys/module/*/drivers
Aka, a singleton, one for each registration

I also disagree with "driver instance" naming in the strongest way, as well.

It does not match what the kernel is doing.

What Gary said.

I personally think DriverData/DeviceData are both acceptable names to me (depends on which aspect you view this from, from device's angle or driver's angle). Probably with a touch of documentation comment that explains this is drvdata in C.
Or, perhaps, we can name it Binding as the relation between driver and device?
The C side is sketchy on naming too, that's probably how we slid into this mess: dev_set_drvdata() is setting per-device data.
How about PerDeviceData?

Ok, let me explain. What we have is sysfs are types of drivers, not instances, e.g.:

/sys/module/psmouse/drivers/serio:psmouse

The type of this driver is serio:psmouse, i.e. a generic driver that can operate PS2 mouse devices.

This serio:psmouse type has methods, such as probe(), remove() etc. Once a matching serial device is connected to the machine, we create an instance of serio:psmouse in probe(). This instance is then stored in the serial device's private data field.
serio:psmouse is the driver, not the type of driver, though.

Also:

$ tree /proc/driver/nvidia
/proc/driver/nvidia
├── capabilities
│   ├── fabric-imex-mgmt
│   ├── gpu0
│   │   └── mig
│   └── mig
│   ├── config
│   └── monitor
├── gpus
│   └── 0000:61:00.0
│   ├── information
│   ├── power
│   └── registry
├── params
├── patches
│   └── README
├── registry
├── suspend
├── suspend_depth
├── version
└── warnings
└── README
Just trying to show that the kernel reports: "a driver has devices", as opposed to "driver instances".
A driver never owns devices.
OK, well however you want to say it, there are multiple devices under a singleton driver, in /proc/driver

When a driver is attached to a device, the device is inserted into the driver’s list of devices.

https://docs.kernel.org/driver-api/driver-model/binding.html
"the driver"
The text, when talking about "drivers", means multiple different kinds of drivers, and not different devices bind to the same data.

John Hubbard said:

OK, well however you want to say it, there are multiple devices under a singleton driver, in /proc/driver

The driver model is not in procfs, this seems to be some nvidia OOT thing, the driver model is represented by sysfs.

In sysfs you do have symbolic links of the devices that are bound to a driver, this binding is what defines an instance.

Gary Guo said:

When a driver is attached to a device, the device is inserted into the driver’s list of devices.

https://docs.kernel.org/driver-api/driver-model/binding.html

The documentation is extremely old and wrong, drivers don't have a list of devices.
Then we should call this binding, not driver.
I think you should not confuse how the code is implemented with the mental model. Of course conceptually the list of devices that the driver has bind to is the "list of devices"
In the mental model there is no list of devices for a driver either.

"The documentation is extremely old and wrong, drivers don't have a list of devices."

I admire this bold claim, but I expect that a patch to change it would fail. WWGS (What Would Greg KH Say)? :)
I don't know, I'm not Greg. :)

$ tree /sys/module/nvidia/drivers
/sys/module/nvidia/drivers
├── pci:nvidia -> ../../../bus/pci/drivers/nvidia
├── pci:nvidia-nvswitch -> ../../../bus/pci/drivers/nvidia-nvswitch
└── platform:nv_platform -> ../../../bus/platform/drivers/nv_platform
A list of driver types in a modules, so?
One item for each type of driver.
I think it's very straightforward that the struct device is named Device, and struct device_driver is named Driver. Anything that deviates from this is just confusion

John Hubbard said:

One item for each type of driver.

This means that the nvidia module implements three driver types. That seems orthogonal to what we are discussing.

Gary Guo said:

I think it's very straightforward that the struct device is named Device, and struct device_driver is named Driver. Anything that deviates from this is just confusion

I don't see why we should deviate from a well-known and working naming convention if we are so insistent on sticking to the C names.
Anyway, enough for today.
If we ever abstract a struct device_driver we can name the surrounding struct Driver, that is fine.

The point is, there are multiple things that we call drivers, i.e. in C we call the structs to register a driver e.g. struct pci_driver, but we also call the implementation of the callbacks of e.g. a struct pci_driver a driver.

For instance, nouveau is a driver (implementation), nova is driver (implementation), etc.

So, let's look at:

struct NovaCore {
    fw: Firmware,
    ...,
}

impl pci::Driver for NovaCore {
    fn probe(pdev: &pci::Device<Core>, _info: &Self::IdInfo) -> impl PinInit<Self, Error> { ... }
}

HereNovaCore is an implementation (or a specific type) of a pci::Driver. When a new pci::Device is now detected in the system then this PCI device is matched with the driver and probe() is called. So, probe() really is the constructor of a driver type. I.e. we create an instance of this driver type, now that we have to store information for this specific device that we have been matched with.

So, probe() then returns an instance of the NovaCoredriver type. This instance represents the binding of the NovaCore driver type and a PCI device, which we also store in this PCI device.

Alternatively - and I think this is what you propose - we could have something like this:

struct NovaCore;

struct NovaCoreData {
    fw: Firmware,
    ...,
}

impl pci::Driver for NovaCore {
    type Data = NovaCoreData;

    fn probe(pdev: &pci::Device<Core>, _info: &Self::IdInfo) -> impl PinInit<Self::Data, Error> { ... }
}

But what does this improve? We now have a useless empty type NovaCore that we use for nothing but implement pci::Driver for. And we represent the instance of the NovaCore driver as a separate NovaCoreData type. Seems pretty useless and eventually people will most likely just end up just doing this:

struct NovaCore {
    fw: Firmware,
    ...,
}

impl pci::Driver for NovaCore {
    type Data = Self;

    fn probe(pdev: &pci::Device<Core>, _info: &Self::IdInfo) -> impl PinInit<Self, Error> { ... }
}

The mental model is that a specific driver, such as NovaCore consists out of shared code (probe(), unbind(), etc.) and per instance data (where a new instance is created for every matched device).

I.e. exactly the pattern of a normal Object: shared code (methods), and per instance data.

This is also exactly the same in C, the driver consists out of shared code, probe() is called for a new instance when a device is matched and the instance is stored in the matched device with dev_set_drvdata().

Maybe a naming change could use Gary's bindings idea, like this:

/// The Nova PCI driver.
pub(crate) struct NovaCore;

/// Per-device data created by probe(), stored in the device's drvdata.
#[pin_data]
pub(crate) struct NovaBinding {
    #[pin]
    pub(crate) gpu: Gpu,
    #[pin]
    _reg: Devres<auxiliary::Registration>,
}

impl pci::Driver for NovaCore {
    type IdInfo = ();
    type Binding = NovaBinding;
    const ID_TABLE: pci::IdTable<Self::IdInfo> = &PCI_TABLE;

    fn probe(pdev: &pci::Device<Core>, _info: &Self::IdInfo) -> impl PinInit<Self::Binding, Error> {
        // ...
    }

    fn unbind(pdev: &pci::Device<Core>, binding: Pin<&Self::Binding>) {
        binding.gpu.unbind(pdev.as_ref());
    }
}

and: 

pub trait Driver: Send {
    type IdInfo: 'static;

    /// The per-device data created by [`Driver::probe`] and stored in the device's `drvdata`.
    ///
    /// This represents the binding between the driver and a specific device. A new instance
    /// is created for each device that matches the driver's ID table.
    type Binding: Send;

    const ID_TABLE: IdTable<Self::IdInfo>;

    fn probe(dev: &Device<device::Core>, id_info: &Self::IdInfo) -> impl PinInit<Self::Binding, Error>;

    fn unbind(dev: &Device<device::Core>, binding: Pin<&Self::Binding>) {
        let _ = (dev, binding);
    }
}

This is exactly what I’ve discussed above already, with the only difference being s/Data/Binding/:

https://rust-for-linux.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/288089-General/topic/Module.2Fdriver-scoped.20data/near/568303773

Danilo Krummrich said:

Alternatively - and I think this is what you propose - we could have something like this:

struct NovaCore;

struct NovaCoreData {
    fw: Firmware,
    ...,
}

impl pci::Driver for NovaCore {
    type Data = NovaCoreData;

    fn probe(pdev: &pci::Device<Core>, _info: &Self::IdInfo) -> impl PinInit<Self::Data, Error> { ... }
}

But what does this improve? We now have a useless empty type NovaCore that we use for nothing but implement pci::Driver for. And we represent the instance of the NovaCore driver as a separate NovaCoreData type. Seems pretty useless and eventually people will most likely just end up just doing this:

struct NovaCore {
    fw: Firmware,
    ...,
}

impl pci::Driver for NovaCore {
    type Data = Self;

    fn probe(pdev: &pci::Device<Core>, _info: &Self::IdInfo) -> impl PinInit<Self, Error> { ... }
}

The idea is that now you actually have a type that represents driver. So if your driver need some data structure shared between multiple device bindings, you put it into the type.

struct NovaCore {
    debugfs: Dir,
}

impl pci::Driver for NovaCore {
    type Data = NovaCoreData;

    fn probe(&self, pdev: &pci::Device<Core>, _info: &Self::IdInfo) -> impl PinInit<Self::Data, Error> {
        // Use self.debugfs
    }
}

This makes creation of the driver singleton explicit -- you create it when you register your driver. Just like when C code creates a pci_driver. It also makes sure that we don't overload the name "driver".
If a driver impl doesn't need anything, good. Then it just do struct MyDriver;, which is quite common in Rust. I don't think we need to "save a type".

I think I can get debugfs change needed by Nova to work with this idea very cleanly. No globals, no debugfs lookups, no failing path. I can work on a prototype next week and we can discuss from there if you're unconvinced.

Gary Guo said:

This makes creation of the driver singleton explicit -- you create it when you register your driver. Just like when C code creates a pci_driver. It also makes sure that we don't overload the name "driver".

The misunderstanding relies in the fact that a struct pci_driver does not define or represent the driver as a whole.

A driver conceptually consists out of much more: the implementation of the bus device callbacks, implementation of the class devices callbacks, IRQ registrations, I/O mappings and other physical device resources. All those are instantiated on a per-device basis.

The struct pci_driver is only the structure a (PCI) driver type is registered through in the driver core. I.e. it does not represent the driver as a whole, but only one single instance of a registration of a driver. So, yes an instance of struct pci_driver is kind of a singleton (at least from the perspective of a certain driver, e.g. nova-core). The only thing you ever do with this structure is to make a specific driver type present in the system by registering it. This is why we have the driver::Registration type in Rust.

(And yes, you could probably argue that struct pci_driver should rather be named struct pci_driver_registration instead.)

But the driver as a whole (e.g. nova-core, nouveau, etc.) is - through its per-device state - instanciated per-device and is not a singleton.

Think about it, when we say "the nouveau driver", we do not only mean the struct pci_driver which is used for registration in the bus and in the driver core. We mean the whole thing, with all of its code, device resources and a per-device state, i.e. a per-device instance.

In ancient times, drivers in the kernel did not have a per-device state and a lot of drivers kept their data in globals, i.e. not even per driver registration, but per module.

One of the big goals of the introduction of the driver-model back then was to fix this by introducing instances of drivers that are created per-device (i.e. a per-device state), such that it became transparent to drivers how many times they are instanciated by the driver core when multiple matching devices were found in the system.

Now, back to your proposal:

What you are proposing to add is per-driver-registration data. This sits quite in between per-module and per-device data. I.e. a module can have multiple driver registrations.

For instance, the nouveau module does have (and the nova-core module will have) a platform driver registration and a PCI driver registration, since the driver can support hardware on both busses.

This shows why the solution of per-driver-registration data is not the solution of the problem. The problem is that we really want to support accessing per-module data. Introducing per-driver-registration data instead is just a workaround.

Surely, you could continue the game and reference count the data and pass it to multiple driver registrations, but what are you doing for modules that do not even connect with the driver core at all, i.e. modules that do not have driver registrations in the first place? One example for this is binder. How can binder access per-module data?
For binder it can still pass the reference around with file ops. AFAIK global is really used for convience

I think in long run, we should:

  • Support self-referential structs with pin-init, so you can reference a field from another
  • Add data to registration, so you can carry a reference to module fields into a registration (or, multiple registrations, e.g. both platform and PCI regs)

In short term though, I am trying to get things work by runtime checks in a form of LazyRevocable, where you it is initialized manually inside module init and revoked before module unload. It does have a slight overhead of needing to check for state, however this shouldn't be an issue in many cases (at least it won't be for Nova and debugfs).

I have a POC branch here: https://github.com/nbdd0121/linux/commits/lazy_revocable_nova_wip/ which I adds LazyRevocable on top of @Timur Tabi 's series. This doesn't work yet, as Revocable today only supports RCU, and for debugfs I believe SRCU is needed, but the design is roughly there.
8 messages were moved from this topic to #pin-init > safe self-referential structs by Benno Lossin.

Gary Guo said:

One idea I have in mind is to have it protected by hazard pointers, so there's no need of RCU at all.

Thanks for the idea. I'd like to try the hazard-pointer approach. Could you outline how you'd use it here?
Note that hazard pointer isn't merged yet: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260223204418.1429025-1-mathieu.desnoyers@efficios.com/

To use hazard pointer for revocable:

On the accessor side:

  1. put pointer into a hazard pointer slot
  2. check that the data is not revoked

On the revoker side:

  1. update revocation flag
  2. perform hazptr_synchronize to wait all users of the pointer to go away
  3. free the revoked data
Thanks for the pointers; I’ll give it a try following this approach.
Notice you can do a call_hazptr() that combine (2 & 3) but asynchronously once we support that.

Also data_ptr shouldn't be needed, you should be able to just protect the data.as_ptr() directly
